data.founder: Jesus Christ (as interpreted by St. Peter and the Apostles)
data.creationMyth: Genesis: Divine creation ex nihilo in six days by the Triune God.
data.coreBeliefs: Holy Trinity, Incarnation, Resurrection, Sacramental Grace, Papal Primacy, Original Sin.
data.functionalPurpose: Redemption from sin and communal life in the Body of Christ via the Sacraments.
data.powerStructure: Strictly Hierarchical (Pope, Cardinals, Bishops, Priests, Deacons, Laity).
data.religiousLineage: Apostolic Succession from St. Peter; roots in Second Temple Judaism.
data.relevanceLegacy: Shaped Western civilization, law, art, and ethics for two millennia.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Papal Infallibility, 2. Real Presence in Eucharist, 3. Marian Intercession, 4. Purgatory, 5. Apostolic Succession.
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Silentium' Protocol - 30 days of silent prayer for 15 minutes before dawn.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Analysis of Agency and Maturity in sacramental participation.
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Managing pride and ritualism; tracking social media's impact on silence.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Gothic Cathedral - Verticality, Light (Stained Glass), and Awe.

quiz.q: Who is considered the first Pope in Catholic tradition?
What was the result of the Council of Nicaea in 325 AD?

flashcards.b: The central sacrament of Catholicism, believed to be the body and blood of Christ.
The doctrine that the Pope is preserved from error when speaking ex cathedra on faith or morals.

data.timePlace: c. 1500-1000 BCE, Ancient Persia (Eastern Iran/Central Asia)
data.creationMyth: Cosmic dualism: Ohrmazd creates light/truth; Ahriman creates darkness/deceit.
data.coreBeliefs: Good Thoughts, Good Words, Good Deeds; Ahura Mazda as the Wise Lord.
data.functionalPurpose: Joining the struggle for Truth (Asha) against Lie (Druj).
data.powerStructure: Maneuvered by Magi/Mobeds (Priests); focus on individual moral agency.
data.religiousLineage: Influenced Judaism, Christianity, and Islam; survives as Parsis in India.
data.relevanceLegacy: Introduced linear time, final judgment, and heaven/hell to global theology.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Fire as Purity, 2. Cosmic Dualism, 3. Final Renewal (Frashokereti), 4. Celestial Hierarchy, 5. Nature Sanctity.
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Asha' Alignment - 30 days of meticulous truth-telling in every interaction.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Evaluating decisions through the lens of Vohu Manah (Good Mind).
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Tracking reactivity to 'pollution' (dishonesty) for one week.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: Atash Behram (Fire Temple) - The architecture of the eternal flame.

quiz.q: Who is the supreme 'Wise Lord' in Zoroastrianism?
What are the three pillars of Zoroastrian ethics?
options: Good Thoughts, Good Words, Good Deeds

flashcards.b: The most sacred portion of the Avesta, hymns composed by Zoroaster himself.
The cosmic principle of truth, order, and righteousness.

data.originCulture: Tribal Arabia; merchants and nomadic Bedouins.
data.creationMyth: God (Allah) created the heavens and earth in six periods; Adam formed from clay.
data.coreBeliefs: Tawhid (Absolute Monotheism), Five Pillars, Prophethood, Final Judgment.
data.functionalPurpose: Submission to the will of God to achieve peace and justice (Ummah).
data.powerStructure: Ulama (Scholars) and Imams (Leaders); decentralized in Sunni, centralized in Shia.
data.religiousLineage: Abrahamic tradition following Ishmael; final revelation after Judaism/Christianity.
data.relevanceLegacy: Global reach, preserved classical knowledge, major political/cultural force.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Finality of Prophethood, 2. Inerrancy of Quran, 3. Predestination (Qadar), 4. Angels/Jinn, 5. Charity (Zakat).
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Salat' Rhythm - 30 days of strict five-time prayer without exception.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Analysis of Agency within the concept of Divine Decree.
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Tracking ego (Nafs) and anger; identifying triggers for spiritual distraction.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Grand Mosque - Geometric infinite patterns and the absence of icons.

quiz.q: What does the word 'Islam' literally mean?

flashcards.b: The central doctrine of the absolute Oneness of God.
The supra-national community of all Muslims.

data.creationMyth: Non-creationist: An endless cycle of cosmic expansion and contraction.
data.coreBeliefs: Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path, Impermanence (Anicca), Nirvana.
data.functionalPurpose: Cessation of suffering (Dukkha) through the elimination of craving.
data.powerStructure: Sangha (Monastic community); varying hierarchies (Dalai Lama, Zen Masters).
data.religiousLineage: Evolved from Sramana traditions; diverged into Theravada, Mahayana, Vajrayana.
data.relevanceLegacy: Pioneered mindfulness, meditation, and psychological introspection globally.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Cycle of Rebirth (Samsara), 2. Karma, 3. No-Self (Anatta), 4. Middle Way, 5. Enlightenment.
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Vipassana' Observation - 30 days of 20-min daily mindfulness.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Awareness vs. Attachment in daily decision making.
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Identifying 'hindrances' (greed, ill-will); tracking emotional reactivity.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Stupa - Symbolic architecture of the enlightened mind.

quiz.q: What is the primary goal of Buddhism?

flashcards.b: The essence of Buddha's teachings: truth of suffering, its cause, its end, and the path to end it.
The Buddhist community, specifically the monastic order.

data.timePlace: Indus Valley (Origins unknown); Vedic Period c. 1500 BCE
data.founder: No single founder; a synthesis of Indo-Aryan and indigenous traditions.
data.sacredText: Vedas, Upanishads, Bhagavad Gita, Ramayana, Mahabharata
data.creationMyth: Cyclical: Brahma creates, Vishnu preserves, Shiva destroys in 'Yugas'.
data.coreBeliefs: Brahman (Ultimate Reality), Atman (Soul), Dharma, Karma, Moksha.
data.functionalPurpose: Realization of the soul's unity with the Divine through various paths (Yogas).
data.religiousLineage: Sanatana Dharma; internal reforms lead to Jainism, Buddhism, Sikhism.
data.relevanceLegacy: World's oldest living religion; profound impact on math, science, and yoga.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Reincarnation, 2. Sacredness of Life (Ahimsa), 3. Multiple Deities/One Truth, 4. Ritual Purity, 5. Moksha.
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Bhakti' Devotion - 30 days of morning mantra meditation.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Swadharma (Individual Duty) vs. Desire-driven action.
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Assessing impacts of 'Maya' (Illusion) on personal goals.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Gopuram (Temple Tower) - Mapping the macrocosm to the microcosm.

options: The cycle of birth and death
Liberation from the cycle of rebirth

quiz.q: Who is the Preserver in the Hindu Trinity (Trimurti)?

flashcards.b: The ultimate, unchanging reality that is the ground of all existence.
The eternal law, duty, or conduct that maintains cosmic order.

data.creationMyth: Genesis 1: God speaks the world into existence; man in God's image.
data.coreBeliefs: Ethical Monotheism, Covenant, Torah Observance, Tikkun Olam (Repairing the World).
data.functionalPurpose: Living as a 'light unto the nations' through adherence to divine law.
data.powerStructure: Non-hierarchical; centered on Rabbis (Scholars) and Community (Minyan).
data.religiousLineage: Parent of Christianity and Islam; continues through Jewish Diaspora.
data.relevanceLegacy: Invented the concept of structural social justice and legal ethics.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Chosenness as Responsibility, 2. Sabbath Rest, 3. Messianic Hope, 4. Oral Tradition, 5. Dietary Laws (Kashrut).
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Shabbat' Disconnect - 30 days of 24h digital detox weekly.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Free will (Bechirah) within the bounds of Mitzvot.
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Identifying 'Loshon Hara' (Negative Speech) patterns for a week.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Western Wall - A ritual return to the center of history.

quiz.q: What is the primary sacred text of Judaism?

flashcards.b: A sacred and binding agreement between God and the Jewish people.
The day of rest, from Friday evening to Saturday evening.

data.timePlace: Indigenous to Ancient Japan (Yayoi period c. 300 BCE)
data.founder: No single founder; evolved from animistic folklore.
data.creationMyth: Izanagi and Izanami stir the ocean; birth of Japanese islands and Amaterasu (Sun).
data.coreBeliefs: Kami (Life Essence), Purity (Harae), Makoto (Sincerity), Natural Harmony.
data.functionalPurpose: Maintaining the state of 'Wa' (Harmony) between humans, nature, and spirits.
data.powerStructure: Shrine Priesthood; historically tied to the Imperial Household.
data.relevanceLegacy: Defines Japanese aesthetics, ethics, and reverence for nature.

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Spirits in Trees/Rocks, 2. Ancestor Veneration, 3. Ritual Cleansing, 4. Seasonal Festivals (Matsuri), 5. Imperial Divinity (historic).
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Misogi' Morning - 30 days of cold water ritual purification.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Individual desire vs. the Harmony of the Great Way.
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Tracking 'Kegare' (spiritual stagnation) through environment clutter.
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Torii Gate - The boundary between the profane and the sacred.

options: Spiritual essences or deities in nature

quiz.q: What is the primary focus of Shinto rituals?

flashcards.b: A traditional Japanese gate most commonly found at the entrance of a Shinto shrine.

data.creationMyth: God (Ik Onkar) created the universe by his command; creation is divine light.
data.coreBeliefs: One God, Equality of Mankind, Selfless Service (Seva), Honest Labor.
data.functionalPurpose: Union with God through truth-living and service to humanity.
data.powerStructure: Democratic equality; authority in the collective Panth and the Scripture.
data.religiousLineage: Succession of ten human Gurus followed by the Eternal Guru (Scripture).
data.relevanceLegacy: Defenders of human rights; pioneers of radical social equality (Langar).

practicum.I: Inherited Beliefs: 1. Ik Onkar (Oneness), 2. Five Ks, 3. Rejection of Caste, 4. Saint-Soldier Ideal, 5. Daily Prayer (Nitnem).
practicum.II: Uncommon Sense: The 'Langar' Service - 30 days of preparing/serving food to strangers.
practicum.III: Requirements of Choice: Assessing agency in the context of Hukam (Divine Will).
practicum.IV: Shadows of Grace: Tracking Five Vices (Ego, Anger, Greed, Attachment, Lust).
practicum.V: Sacred Space: The Golden Temple - Four doors open to all directions/castes.

quiz.q: Who was the first Guru of Sikhism?

options: A communal kitchen serving free meals to all

flashcards.b: The symbol representing the Oneness of God.
